title: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland
description: A children's fantasy of nonsense logic, verbal play, and destabilized authority.
descriptionParagraphs: A children's fantasy of nonsense logic, verbal play, and destabilized authority.

Alice's Adventures in Wonderland is usually read through its treatment of children's literature, fantasy, and satire. As a children's novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes children's literature, fantasy, and satire feel immediate.

cover.alt: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A children's fantasy of nonsense logic, verbal play, and destabilized authority.
banningParagraphs: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland entered censorship debates as a children's novel associated with children's literature, fantasy, and satire. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around anthropomorphic animals and moral panic.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1931 in China, where Hunan provincial authorities banned circulation. Officials reportedly objected to animals speaking and behaving like people. Whether comic or absurd, the case still illustrates how ideology can turn whimsy into a problem.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.
